query: definition of strain in biomechanics and medicine

retrieved_docs.title: Prosthetic Heart Valve -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: As the heart continuously beat throughout a lifetime, there is cyclic stress and strain on the native heart valves.  The left side of the heart, in particular, is associated with higher pressures which range between approximately 100 mmHg to 150 mmHg.  As a result, the left-sided valves (i.e., aortic and mitral) have a higher predisposition to disease compared to their right-sided counterparts. A diseased valve that can cause hemodynamic compromise or affect the quality of life will often warrant evaluation regarding possible prosthesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Vehicle Collisions -- Definition/Introduction -- Mechanism of Injury
retrieved_docs.content: According to the Law of Conservation of Energy, “Energy can neither be destroyed or created, but it can change form.” This law goes to the essence of trauma. The kinetic or moving energy must be transferred onto the object impacted. So, if a vehicle is traveling at a high rate of speed when it impacts an object, the energy force can be quadrupled. Spinal injuries may be a result of axial loads. [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Musculoskeletal Examination -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Observation begins with accessing any visible gross abnormalities of skin and other components of the musculoskeletal system. Palpation uses from light to firm pressure to identify and quantify the abnormalities of the musculoskeletal system, pain/tenderness, trigger points. Normal or abnormal findings that could be elicited by observation and palpation include: symmetry/asymmetry - skin color and appearance, rash, ulcers, lack of sweating hair abnormalities - warmth and heat - Swelling including effusions, nodules, and inflammatory findings like synovial and periarticular thickening - muscle atrophy, tone, contractures, and spasms - crepitations - Joint deformities including spine like kyphosis and scoliosis. Manipulation consists of different techniques to access the range of motion (ROM), strength, sensations, reflexes, and gait. The proper evaluation consists mainly of testing strength (evaluate individually the muscle capacity and integrity), range of motion (evaluate the joint independently, it's restrictions, and hypo or hypermobility), reflex and sensory function (evaluate dermatomes, reflex and sensory function, to identify possibles correlations and dysfunctions between musculoskeletal and neural system), gait analysis (evaluate the integrated functions of locomotion), and trigger points (to evaluate myofascial pain, presence of trigger points and association with patient symptoms).

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Ankle Sprain -- History and Physical -- Grading of Ankle Sprains
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, acute ankle sprains are graded by severity to guide management and prognosis. Grade I (mild) This involves a slight stretching of the ligament with microscopic fiber tearing. The patient exhibits mild tenderness, minimal swelling, and no mechanical instability. Typically, weight-bearing is possible with only mild pain. Grade II (moderate) This involves an incomplete ligament tear. There is moderate pain, swelling, and bruising with tenderness over the involved structures. The patient may have some difficulty with weight-bearing. On exam, the ligament is somewhat lax compared to the opposite ankle, but a definite endpoint to stress testing is still present. Grade III (severe)

retrieved_docs.title: Brudzinski Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Brudzinski's sign is characterized by reflexive flexion of the knees and hips following passive neck flexion. To elicit this sign, the examiner places one hand on the patient's chest and the other hand behind the patient's neck. The examiner then passively flexes the neck forward and assesses whether the knees and hips flex. Upon passive neck flexion, a positive test results when the patient flexes his knees and hips. [3] During the process of performing the test, the hand on the chest prevents the patient from reflexively lifting his chest off the bed which decreases the specificity of the test. While the pathophysiology for the hip/knee flexion is not completely understood, the theory is that hip and knee flexion occurs as an involuntary reflex to compensate and help reduce meningeal irritation. Passive neck flexion causes spinal cord movement and stretching of the meninges, resulting in pain for patients with meningitis. The thinking is that the involuntary hip/knee flexion occurs to create maximal relaxation of the meninges, reducing pain. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Vehicle Collisions -- Definition/Introduction -- Mechanism of Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Energy transfer from the environment can result from a variety of causes. The intensity of the energy transfer will cause damage to the surrounding tissues, organs, muscles, and other body structures when it exceeds the body’s ability to resist such rapid, forceful energy changes.

retrieved_docs.title: Modified Ashworth Scale -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The assessment begins with the joint in either a fully flexed or fully extended position, depending on the muscle group being tested. The examiner should passively move the limb through its full range of motion at a controlled speed, ideally completing the movement within approximately 1 second. The movement should be performed smoothly and without excessive force.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Shoulder Instability -- Pathophysiology -- Long-term Implications
retrieved_docs.content: By definition, dislocation arthropathy describes degenerative changes of the shoulder joint. Hovelius and Rahme conducted a prospective study of 257 primary anterior shoulder dislocations in 255 patients. At a 25-year follow-up, 27% had mild glenohumeral arthritis, and 34% had moderate to severe arthrosis. [39] Although the role of recurrent dislocations is unclear, the pathophysiology underlying the development of dislocation arthropathy may be related to acute changes during the primary or recurrent traumatic instability events, the ensuing altered glenohumeral joint biomechanics, and/or attritional changes that occur secondary to the recurrent instability.

retrieved_docs.title: Musculoskeletal Examination -- Definition/Introduction -- Strength
retrieved_docs.content: To evaluate strength, the Medical Research Council scale of muscle strength (MCR-scale) is commonly used that grades the strength into 0 to 5 [4] : 0 – No contraction 1 – Flicker or trace of contraction 2 – Full range of active movement, with gravity eliminated 3 – Active movement against gravity 4 – Active movement against gravity and resistance 5 – Normal power

retrieved_docs.title: Trendelenburg Sign -- Definition/Introduction -- Prerequisites of Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The patient can stand on the affected side for more than 30 seconds The patient can understand commands The patient has intact coordination The patient has free abduction movement; no fixed adduction deformity

retrieved_docs.title: Fracture Healing Overview -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The type of fracture healing is governed by the achieved mechanical stability at the fracture site and, consequently, the strain. An appropriate mechanical stimulation, such as strain, facilitates tissue formation at the bony ends. The amount of the involved strain dictates the biological behavior of the cells involved in the healing process and, consequently, the type of bone healing. [2] [3] [4] Primary bone healing ensues with mechanical strain below 2%, whereas secondary bone healing ensues when the mechanical strain is between 2 and 10%. [5] [6] [7] [8] In contrast, a strain >10% results in non-union or delayed union. [9] [10] [6] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Fitness for Duty and Return to Work -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the European Union, the European Accessibility Act is equivalent to the ADA. Other regions have enacted requirements that address similar issues (see Table 1. Examples of Governmental Requirements Enacted in Various Nations).

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Vehicle Collisions -- Definition/Introduction -- Mechanism of Injury
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanism of injury (MOI) specifically refers to the method by which trauma and its associated forces directly or indirectly impacts the human body.  MOI implies a specific transfer of energy from one source (i.e. the environment) to another (i.e. the human body). These injury patterns assist in the determination of the extent of an injury and fundamental decision-making process regarding trauma triage guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Physical Principles And Instrumentation -- Definition/Introduction -- Instrumentation
retrieved_docs.content: The linear transducer does not directly utilize every crystal, but each element is electrically stimulated or vibrated and subsequently excites adjacent crystals. Each crystal within the linear transducer is not given direct electrical stimulation. The advantage of the linear probe is a larger field of view and a higher superficial focus. Common clinical applications of this probe are within the soft tissue and more superficial structures. See Image. Ultrasound Linear Probe.

retrieved_docs.title: Musculoskeletal Examination -- Definition/Introduction -- Range of Motion (ROM)
retrieved_docs.content: ROM could be either active or passive. An active ROM is patient-initiated, which can access not only joint mobility but also an intact musculoskeletal and nervous system. Passive ROM examination is by initiating manipulation of the joint. ROM depends on the type of joint, and also it is important to know whether ROM is limited due to pain or guarding, weakness, or muscle or joint disease. Comparing to the unaffected side is indispensable. The assessment of a range of motion needs to be quantified (to avoid subjectivity bias), and for this, the use of a goniometer is indispensable. There are two types of goniometers; the first one is to use the universal goniometer and manually scale the ROM. [2] The second is to use the smartphone goniometric application. It has indications for greater precision metrics then the universal goniometer. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Williams Back Exercises -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A series of exercises recommended for Williams flexion exercises includes pelvic tilt, single knee-to-chest motion, double knee-to-chest motion, partial sit-up, hamstring stretch, hip flexor stretch, and squats. These exercises aim to reduce lower back pain, strengthen the core muscles, and restore functional mobility. In addition, preventing future injuries and the development of chronic pain is crucial. In an 8-week study comparing Williams back pain exercises to a no-treatment control group, participants in the exercise group were found to have decreased back pain and an increase in the flexibility of their hamstring, hip flexor, and lumbar extensor muscles. These participants were also found to have increased abdominal muscle strength. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2108
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” aggregates a range of injury‑related concepts. It includes the category “sprains and strains,” the condition “الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة الوتر” (late effects of tendon injury), the condition “التأثير المتأخر للبتر الرضحي” (late effect of traumatic amputation), and the specific injury terms “بتر رضحي في الإصبع” (traumatic amputation of the finger) and “بتر رضحي في القدم” (traumatic amputation of the foot). These relationships demonstrate that the “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” serves as a comprehensive grouping for various accident and injury events, supporting systematic identification, classification, and analysis of such medical conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2180
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” (Accidents and Injuries) serves as an overarching classification that encompasses a range of injury‑related concepts. Within this SMQ, sprains and strains are included as common musculoskeletal injuries. The delayed effect of traumatic amputation (“التأثير المتأخر للبتر الرضحي”) is also categorized under this SMQ, highlighting the long‑term sequelae that can follow severe limb loss. Specific forms of traumatic amputation are further organized as sub‑members of the SMQ, including traumatic amputation of the limbs (“بتر رضحي في الأطراف”), of the arm (“بتر رضحي في الذراع”), and of the hand (“بتر رضحي في اليد”). By grouping these entities together, the SMQ provides a structured framework for understanding the relationships among various types of accidents and injuries, facilitating accurate documentation, diagnosis, and management in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1798
retrieved_docs.content: The Standardized MedDRA Query “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” serves as a comprehensive grouping for a range of injury‑related concepts. Within this SMQ, the term “sprains and strains” is included as a member, reflecting its relevance to accident‑related musculoskeletal trauma. The SMQ also incorporates “الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة الوتر” (late effects of tendon injury), linking chronic tendon sequelae to the broader category of accidents and injuries. In addition, the SMQ is associated with the specific injury categories “بتر رضحي في الأطراف” (traumatic amputation of the limbs), “بتر رضحي في الذراع” (traumatic amputation of the arm), and “بتر رضحي في اليد” (traumatic amputation of the hand), indicating that these forms of severe limb loss are captured under the same accident‑injury framework. By aggregating sprains, tendon‑related late effects, and various forms of traumatic amputation, the “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” provides clinicians and researchers with a unified reference for identifying, monitoring, and managing adverse events that arise from accidental injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2136
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” serves as a comprehensive grouping for a variety of injury‑related concepts. Sprains and strains are included within this SMQ, indicating that these common musculoskeletal injuries are classified under the broader category of accidents and injuries. The late effects of tendon injury (“الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة الوتر”) are also members of the SMQ, reflecting that chronic sequelae of tendon damage are captured in the same classification. Likewise, the late effect of traumatic amputation (“التأثير المتأخر للبتر الرضحي”) belongs to the SMQ, linking delayed complications of severe limb loss to the overall injury framework. Specific forms of traumatic amputation are further represented: traumatic amputation in the limbs (“بتر رضحي في الأطراف”) and traumatic amputation in the finger (“بتر رضحي في الإصبع”) are both categorized as members of the SMQ. Together, these entities illustrate that the “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” encompasses both acute injuries such as sprains and strains and chronic or late‑stage consequences of more severe injuries, providing an integrated structure for understanding and managing a wide spectrum of accident‑related medical conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2204
retrieved_docs.content: Other OR therapeutic procedures on musculoskeletal system is a classification term that categorizes other or therapeutic procedures on the musculoskeletal system, grouping together various interventions aimed at treating musculoskeletal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2417
retrieved_docs.content: The Standardized MedDRA Query (SMQ) titled “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” aggregates a broad spectrum of injury‑related medical concepts. Within this SMQ, sprains and strains are included as representative musculoskeletal injuries. The SMQ also encompasses “الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة الوتر” (late effects of tendon injury), reflecting the sequelae that can follow tendon trauma. In addition, the SMQ groups together specific traumatic amputations, namely “بتر رضحي في الذراع” (amputation of the arm), “بتر رضحي في القدم” (amputation of the foot), and “بتر رضحي في اليد” (amputation of the hand). By classifying these entities under the “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)”, the query provides a comprehensive framework for identifying and monitoring a variety of accident‑ and injury‑related adverse events in clinical and pharmacovigilance settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2222
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ titled “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” serves as a comprehensive grouping for a variety of injury‑related concepts. It includes the category “sprains and strains,” indicating that sprain and strain injuries are classified within this SMQ. The late sequelae of tendon injury, expressed as “الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة الوتر,” are also incorporated as a member of the SMQ. In addition, several forms of traumatic amputation are organized under this SMQ, specifically “بتر رضحي في الأطراف” (traumatic amputation of limbs), “بتر رضحي في الإصبع” (traumatic amputation of a finger), “بتر رضحي في القدم” (traumatic amputation of the foot), and “بتر رضحي في اليد” (traumatic amputation of the hand). By grouping these entities together, the SMQ provides a structured framework that aids clinicians and researchers in capturing, analyzing, and managing data related to accidents and injuries, thereby supporting more systematic patient care and epidemiological monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2286
retrieved_docs.content: The Standardized MedDRA Query titled “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” functions as a comprehensive grouping for a range of injury‑related concepts. It includes “sprains and strains” as a member, as well as “الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة الوتر” (late effects of tendon injury). In addition, the SMQ encompasses specific partial amputations: “بتر رضحي في إصبع القدم” (partial amputation of the toe), “بتر رضحي في الإصبع” (partial amputation of a finger), and “بتر رضحي في اليد” (partial amputation of the hand). These relationships demonstrate that the “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” aggregates diverse accident and injury terms, supporting systematic identification and analysis of related adverse events in clinical and pharmacovigilance contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2211
retrieved_docs.content: The SMQ “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” serves as a comprehensive collection of injury‑related concepts. Sprains and strains are included as members of this SMQ, indicating that they are classified under accidents and injuries. The delayed effect of traumatic amputation (“التأثير المتأخر للبتر الرضحي”) is also a member of the SMQ, linking it to the broader category of injury events. Specific forms of traumatic amputation—“بتر رضحي في الأطراف” (amputation in limbs), “بتر رضحي في اليد” (amputation in the hand), and “بتر رضحي في الإصبع” (amputation in the finger)—are each listed as members of the SMQ. Together, these entities illustrate that the “الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ)” aggregates various types of accidents and injuries, facilitating consistent identification, reporting, and analysis of these conditions in medical practice and research.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 5 -- Community 2961
retrieved_docs.content: The designation “Allowed qualifier” is used as an etiological label for several serious medical conditions, indicating that it denotes the underlying cause of death (التموت), lower‑extremity ulceration (قرحات الطرفين السفليين), and femoral‑neck fracture (كسر عنق الفخذ). Recognizing “Allowed qualifier” as the causal descriptor for these outcomes helps clinicians classify and communicate the origins of these adverse health events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, and crushing injury or internal injury is in turn associated with the Arabic term “إصابة,” which means injury; thus, when a condition is described as “due to” a crushing or internal injury, it denotes a direct causal link to an injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2348
retrieved_docs.content: Sprains and strains are classified within the broader injury grouping identified as الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ). Within this same SMQ, the category الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة الوتر (late effects of tendon injury) is also included, reflecting the spectrum of musculoskeletal sequelae that can follow acute trauma. In addition, the SMQ encompasses several specific forms of traumatic amputation: بتر رضحي في الأطراف (amputation of the limbs), بتر رضحي في الذراع (amputation of the arm), and بتر رضحي في الساق (amputation of the leg). By organizing sprains and strains, late tendon‑injury effects, and the various limb‑amputation subtypes under the unified framework of الحوادث والإصابات (SMQ), clinicians and coders can systematically capture the full range of accident‑related injuries, facilitating accurate documentation, epidemiologic tracking, and comprehensive patient management.
